Learn React Sooner or Later, So Why Not Now? - React Advanced - Custom Hooks - (17)

In the earlier chapters we got to know a bunch of React Hooks, but every single one of them was provided by React itself. So what if you want to roll your own Hook — is that even allowed? That's exactly what this post is about: getting to know the Custom Hook side of React Hooks.
So what is a Custom Hook? Before we get to that, let me bring up something else first.

When you're actually building stuff, you run into repeated logic all the time. And when that happens, the natural move is to pull that repeated chunk out into its own function so you can reuse it and maintain it in one place.
Why am I bringing this up? Because a React Custom Hook is basically doing this exact thing for us. Which probably leaves you with one question: "Okay, but what actually
Honestly, it's just a JavaScript function. The only difference is that it uses a React Hook inside it. That's it.
There's one basic rule you have to follow when writing a Custom Hook: the function name has to start with
, and the rest of the name usually starts with a capital letter — exactly like the other Hooks we've already met:
You can see that every React Hook in that list is
+ a capitalized name. So when we write our own Custom Hook, we follow the same rule.
Next up I'll walk through a couple of simple Custom Hook examples, which should give you a better feel for how to write and use them.

From these two simple examples, you can see how handy Custom Hooks are for building your own Hooks. On top of that, each Custom Hook has its own state, so you don't have to worry about it stepping on any other state.
Of course, these are just a few simple Custom Hooks. If you want to see more examples like these, check out
— there are tons of different Custom Hooks on there to learn from.

We've met a bunch of React Hooks in the earlier chapters, but every one of them was provided by React itself. So what if you want to build your own Hook? In this post we'll get to know Custom Hooks — what they actually are, the one rule you have to follow, and a couple of small examples (useKeyDown and useLog) to make it click.
